chunks.section: Introduction: What Are We Trying to Do?
chunks.subsection: The Core Question of This Module
chunks.tex_text:
Can we make better use of data to improve predictions about financial outcomes?

Examples we'll tackle in this course:

• Which stocks will outperform next month and why?
• Will this borrower default on their loan and why?

The challenge: Financial markets are noisy, correlations and volatility change over time, and many claimed patterns do not hold up out-of-sample.

chunks.section: Introduction: What Are We Trying to Do?
chunks.subsection: A Concrete Example: Predicting Stock Returns
chunks.tex_text:
Setup: You're a portfolio manager trying to pick winning stocks

Data-Rich Environment

• Company financials: earnings, book value, debt levels
• Market data: past returns, trading volume, volatility
• Valuation ratios: price-to-earnings, price-to-book
• Momentum: how has the stock performed recently?

What Do You Want to Predict?

• Next month's stock return (or just: will it beat the market?)

The Machine Learning (ML) Approach:

• Collect historical data on stock characteristics and returns
• Learn the relationship between characteristics and subsequent returns
• Apply this relationship to make predictions as new data on characteristics arrive.

chunks.section: Introduction: What Are We Trying to Do?
chunks.subsection: Learning from Data $\neq$ Fitting the Data
chunks.tex_text:
& Econometrics & Machine Learning

Primary goal & Understand why & Predict what

Model structure & Often theory-driven & Mostly data-driven

Variables & Few, interpretable & Many, algorithm selects

Focus & Statistical significance & Out-of-sample accuracy

Punchline: ML for prediction, econometrics for causal understanding. Good practitioners combine both as much as possible.

Let's establish clear notation we will use throughout:

• $x$ = features (the information we use to predict)

• For a single observation: $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_p)$
• Example: size, value ratio, momentum, profitability

• $y$ = target (what we're trying to predict)

• Example: next month's return

• $n$ = number of observations (e.g., stock-months)
• $p$ = number of features

Our data structure: We observe $n$ examples:
\[ (x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}), \; (x^{(2)}, y^{(2)}), \; \ldots, \; (x^{(n)}, y^{(n)}) \]

Each $x^{(i)} = (x_1^{(i)}, \ldots, x_p^{(i)})$ is a vector of $p$ features; each $y^{(i)}$ is the outcome.

chunks.subsection: The Goal: Learn a Prediction Rule
chunks.tex_text:
We want to find a function $f$ that predicts $Y$ from $X$ :
\[ \text{Predicted } Y = \widehat{f}(X) \]
N.B., $\widehat{f}$ signifies “an estimate of the unknown $f$ ”.

Concretely:

$f(\text{size, value, momentum, ...}) = \text{predicted return}$

What kind of function?

• Linear: $f(X) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots$ (Lecture 2, 5)
• Decision trees: If size $>$ threshold, then... (Lecture 3, 6)
• Neural networks: Complex nonlinear combinations (Lecture 9, if time permits)

The challenge: Learn an $f$ from historical data such that it works well on new, unseen data.

chunks.subsection: Why Prediction Is Hard: The Noise Problem
chunks.tex_text:
Reality: Even with perfect information, we cannot predict $y$ exactly.

We can think of the data as generated by:
\[ y = \underbrace{f(x)}_{\text{predictable part}} + \underbrace{\epsilon}_{\text{unpredictable noise}} \]

What is $\epsilon$ ? Everything affecting $y$ that is NOT in our features $x$ :

• Information we don't have access to (e.g., private order flow)
• Events that occur after we make our prediction
• Measurement error in reported financials
• Pure randomness in how markets aggregate information

Key insight:

• $f(x)$ is what we can hope to learn (the “signal”)
• $\epsilon$ is irreducible given our information set
• Better features can shrink $\epsilon$ , but never eliminate it

chunks.subsection: How Good Is Our Prediction? Measuring Error
chunks.tex_text:
Question: What do we mean by “as accurately as possible”?

Prediction error for a single observation:
\[ \text{Error} = y - \widehat{f}(x) = \text{Actual} - \text{Predicted} \]

Problem: Errors can be positive or negative, so they cancel out

Solution: Square the errors (penalizes big mistakes more)
\[ \text{Squared Error} = (y - \widehat{f}(x))^2 \]

Average over all observations:
\[ \text{Mean Squared Error (MSE)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y^{(i)} - \widehat{f}(x^{(i)}))^2 \]

Lower MSE = More accurate predictions

Step 1: Choose a family of possible prediction functions

• Linear models, trees, neural networks, etc.

Step 2: Find the $\widehat{f}$ that minimizes prediction error on training data
\[ \widehat{f} = \argmin_{f} \; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(y^{(i)} - f(x^{(i)})\right)^2 \]

Step 3: Evaluate on new data (we'll discuss this more soon)

The hat notation $\widehat{f}$ means “our estimate of $f$ ” — it's what we learn from data. The true $f$ is unknown!

chunks.section: The Fundamental Tradeoff: Bias vs. Variance
chunks.tex_text:
If we minimize error on our training data,

will predictions be good on new, unseen data?

Spoiler: Not necessarily!

Two distinct concepts:

• Training error: How well does $\widehat{f}$ fit the data we used to build it?
• Test error: How well does $\widehat{f}$ predict on new data?

Training error can be made arbitrarily small by using a sufficiently complex model. But this doesn't mean test error will be small!

chunks.section: The Fundamental Tradeoff: Bias vs. Variance
chunks.tex_text:
Two sources of prediction error on new data:

0.48
1. Bias (Underfitting)

• Model too simple
• Misses real patterns
• Training error $\approx$ Test error
• Both higher than necessary

Example: Predicting returns using only firm size, when momentum and value also matter

0.48
2. Variance (Overfitting)

• Model too complex
• Fits noise, not just signal
• Training error $<<$ Test error
• Gap signals overfitting

Example: Using 200 characteristics to predict returns with only 500 observations

Reducing bias (more complex model) typically increases variance, and vice versa.

chunks.section: The Fundamental Tradeoff: Bias vs. Variance
chunks.tex_text:
Thought experiment: What if we could repeat our analysis with different training samples?

• Bullseye = true relationship $f(X)$ we are trying to learn
• Each dart = prediction from model trained on a different sample
• Bias: How far is the average prediction from the truth?
• Variance: How much do predictions scatter across samples?

chunks.section: The Fundamental Tradeoff: Bias vs. Variance
chunks.tex_text:
• Left: Straight line misses the curvature in the data
• Middle: Smooth curve captures the overall pattern
• Right: Wiggly curve passes through every point — but will fail on new data

chunks.section: The Fundamental Tradeoff: Bias vs. Variance
chunks.tex_text:
Mathematical fact: The expected test error decomposes into three parts
\[ \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[(Y - \widehat{f}(X))^2\right]}_{\text{What we care about}} = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2}_{\text{How Wrong is $\widehat{f}$}} + \underbrace{\text{Variance}}_{\text{How Unstable is $\widehat{f}$}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible noise}} \]

• $\sigma^2$ is the inherent unpredictability — even the best model can't eliminate it
• We control Bias and Variance through model choice
• Minimizing test error requires balancing Bias and Variance

& Simple Model & Complex Model

Bias & High & Low

Variance & Low & High

chunks.section: The Fundamental Tradeoff: Bias vs. Variance
chunks.subsection: Complexity vs. Error: The Classic Picture
chunks.tex_text:
Key observation:

• Training error always decreases with complexity
• Test error has a U-shape — there's a sweet spot
• We must estimate test error to choose optimal complexity

chunks.section: The Fundamental Tradeoff: Bias vs. Variance
chunks.subsection: Why Overfitting Is Deadly in Finance
chunks.tex_text:
Finance is especially prone to overfitting because:

• Signal-to-noise ratio is very low (markets are near-efficient)
• Many candidate predictors (300+ characteristics documented)
• Limited independent observations (monthly data = 12 per year)
• Predictive relationships may change over time (regime shifts)

The evidence (Harvey, Liu & Zhu, 2016):

• Surveyed 300+ factors published in top finance journals
• Most factors that “worked” in-sample do not replicate
• Key culprit: researchers test many strategies, publish only winners

If it seems too good to be true, it is probably overfitting

We need to estimate test error, but we only have training data!

Naive approach: Use training error

• Problem: Training error is too optimistic (see previous discussion)
• Complex models can achieve zero training error while being useless

Solution: Hold out some data for validation

• Don't use it for training
• Use it to estimate how well we would do on new data

The data you use to evaluate a model must be different from the data you used to build it

Standard approach: Divide your data into three parts

• Training set: Fit different models
• Validation set: Compare models, choose the best one
• Test set: Evaluate final model (touch only once!)

Why three sets?

• Validation set gets “used up” as we try different models
• Test set gives honest final assessment

Problem: Holding out 40% of data reduces training sample

Solution: K-Fold Cross-Validation

Procedure:

• Split data into K equal parts (“folds”)
• For each fold: train on other K-1 folds, validate on this fold
• Average the K validation errors

Common choice: K = 5 or K = 10

chunks.subsection: Problem: Random CV Doesn't Work for Time Series!
chunks.tex_text:
Financial data is ordered in time. Random splits cause problems:

This creates look-ahead bias:

• Training on 2020 data to “predict” 2018 returns
• Model learns from information that would not exist at prediction time
• Overly optimistic performance estimates

Solution: Only use past data to predict future data (Walk-Forward Validation)

Two variants:

• Expanding window: Use all past data (shown above)
• Rolling window: Fixed-size training window (discards old data)

chunks.subsection: The Golden Rule of Out-of-Sample Testing
chunks.tex_text:
Once you look at test set results, the test set is contaminated.

You get one shot at honest out-of-sample evaluation.

Why?

• If you adjust model selection/estimation based on test results, you are implicitly fitting to the test set
• This is exactly the overfitting problem we are trying to avoid
• Performance will be overestimated in a realistic setting

Best practice:

• Do all model selection on training + validation data
• Lock in your final model choice
• Only then evaluate on test set
• Report the test result, even if disappointing

chunks.section: Common Pitfalls in Financial Machine Learning
chunks.subsection: Why Financial ML Is Especially Hard
chunks.tex_text:
Recall from previous slides: Overfitting is deadly in finance.

Three additional pitfalls specific to financial applications:

• Look-ahead bias — Using information you would not have at prediction time

• Survivorship bias — Only analyzing firms/funds that survived

• Data snooping — Testing many strategies, reporting only winners

Each of these makes backtested performance look better than reality.

A strategy can fail for reasons beyond overfitting — even a properly validated model can “be garbage in, garbage out.”

chunks.section: Common Pitfalls in Financial Machine Learning
chunks.tex_text:
Definition: Using information that would not have been available at the time the prediction was made.

Common sources:

• Timing of data releases: Using Q4 earnings announced in February to “predict” January returns
• Restated financials: Using corrected accounting data, not original reports
• Point-in-time vs. revised data: Economic indicators (e.g., GDP, Inflation, Unemployment) are often revised months later
• Index membership: Using current S&P 500 constituents for a 1990 backtest

Example:

• You build a model using book value reported in Compustat
• But Compustat shows the final value, not what was known at the first-time release
• Your model “knows” things investors could not have known

chunks.section: Common Pitfalls in Financial Machine Learning
chunks.subsection: Look-Ahead Bias: How to Avoid It
chunks.tex_text:
Best practices:

• Use point-in-time databases when available

• CRSP, Compustat with proper date alignment
• IBES for analyst forecasts with announcement dates

• Apply realistic lags

• Quarterly data: available $\approx$ 45 days after quarter end
• Annual data: available $\approx$ 90 days after fiscal year end

• Be explicit about timing

• Document when each feature was knowable/observable
• Build this into your data pipeline

• Use walk-forward validation (as discussed in previous slides)

When in doubt, add one (or more) lag(s). Being conservative hurts less than look-ahead bias.

chunks.section: Common Pitfalls in Financial Machine Learning
chunks.tex_text:
Definition: Analyzing only entities that “survived” to the present, ignoring those that failed, delisted, or disappeared.

Why it matters:

• Failed companies often had distinct characteristics before failure
• Excluding them biases your sample toward “winners”
• Relationships estimated on survivors may not hold in the full population, which also includes the “losers”

Classic examples:

• Mutual funds: Databases often drop funds that close; remaining funds look better on average
• Hedge funds: Voluntary reporting + fund closures = severe upward bias
• Stocks: Using only current exchange members misses delistings

chunks.section: Common Pitfalls in Financial Machine Learning
chunks.subsection: Survivorship Bias: How to Avoid It
chunks.tex_text:
Best practices:

• Use survivorship-bias-free databases

• CRSP includes delisted stocks with delisting returns
• Check if your data source retains “dead” entities

• Include delisting returns

• When a stock delists, there is often a final return (often negative)
• Ignoring this overstates strategy performance

• Be skeptical of “cleaned” datasets

• Sometimes cleaning removes exactly the observations you need
• Understand what was removed and why

• For credit risk: Ensure you include loans/mortgages that defaulted, not just those that were fully or partly repaid

chunks.section: Common Pitfalls in Financial Machine Learning
chunks.tex_text:
Definition: Testing many hypotheses/strategies on the same data and selectively reporting the winners.

The problem:

• With 100 random strategies, $\sim$ 5 will appear significant at the 5% level by chance
• If you only report the winners, they look real
• This happens implicitly through researcher degrees of freedom

Forms of data snooping:

• Trying many feature combinations, keeping what works
• Testing multiple model specifications
• Optimizing hyperparameters on the same data used for evaluation
• “Peeking” at test data to guide model development

Harvey, Liu & Zhu (2016): Many published factors are likely false discoveries due to collective data snooping across the literature.

chunks.section: Common Pitfalls in Financial Machine Learning
chunks.subsection: Data Snooping: How to Mitigate It
chunks.tex_text:
No perfect solution, but several defenses:

• Adjust for multiple testing

• Bonferroni correction: divide significance level by number of tests
• More sophisticated: Benjamini-Hochberg for false discovery rate
• Chordia, Goyal, and Saretto (2020): you need to adjust for portfolio return correlations.

• Pre-registration

• Commit to methodology before seeing results
• Common in medicine, emerging in finance

• True out-of-sample testing

• Hold out data you genuinely never look at
• Or use new data that arrives after model development

• Economic plausibility

• Does the pattern make sense? Is there a “story” to be told?
• Pure statistical patterns without economic rationale are suspect

chunks.section: Common Pitfalls in Financial Machine Learning
chunks.subsection: Summary: A Checklist for Honest Backtests
chunks.tex_text:
Before trusting any ML strategy in finance, verify:

• [ ] No look-ahead bias

• All features use only information available at prediction time

• [ ] No survivorship bias

• Sample includes failed/delisted entities

• [ ] Proper validation

• Time-series CV, not random splits
• Clean separation of train/validation/test

• [ ] Multiple testing adjustment

• Account for all strategies tried, not just the winner

• [ ] Economic sensibility

• Results have plausible interpretation

If a study does not address these, treat results with skepticism.

Python has become the dominant language for ML in finance:

• Rich ecosystem of ML libraries
• Strong data manipulation tools
• Good integration with databases and APIs
• Large community and documentation
• Used by major quant firms and fintech companies

Alternatives:

• R: Strong for statistics, less so for production deployment
• Julia: Fast, but smaller ecosystem
• MATLAB: Common in academia, expensive, less ML support

This course: All implementation in Python using industry-standard libraries

Library & Purpose & Key Use

numpy & Numerical computing & Arrays, linear algebra

pandas & Data manipulation & DataFrames, time series

scikit-learn & Machine learning & Models, pipelines, CV

matplotlib & Visualization & Basic plots

seaborn & Visualization & Statistical graphics

xgboost & Gradient boosting & Tree ensembles

lightgbm & Gradient boosting & Fast tree ensembles

shap & Interpretability & Model explanations

Focus: We use scikit-learn as our primary ML library.

• Consistent API across all models
• Built-in cross-validation and pipelines
• Good documentation and examples

All scikit-learn models follow the same pattern:

verbatim
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge

# 1. Create model with hyperparameters
model = Ridge(alpha=1.0)

# 2. Fit to training data
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# 3. Make predictions
predictions = model.predict(X_test)

# 4. Evaluate
score = model.score(X_test, y_test)
verbatim

This works for any model: Lasso, RandomForestRegressor, etc.

Once you learn this pattern, switching between models is trivial.

Real ML workflows have multiple steps: (1) Handle missing values and outliers, (2) Scale features and pre-processing, and (3) Fit model

Pipelines bundle these together:

verbatim
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer

pipeline = Pipeline([
('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='median')),
('scaler', StandardScaler()),
('model', Ridge(alpha=1.0))
])

pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
predictions = pipeline.predict(X_test)
verbatim

Benefits: Prevents data leakage, easy to save/deploy, cleaner code

Scikit-learn provides TimeSeriesSplit:

verbatim
from sklearn.model_selection import TimeSeriesSplit
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

# Create time-series splitter
tscv = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5)

# Cross-validate respecting time order
scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=tscv)

print(f"Mean CV Score: scores.mean():.4f ")
verbatim

Implementing the walk-forward validation from previous slides becomes trivial in Python.

Note: Data must be sorted by time before using TimeSeriesSplit!

chunks.section: A Bird's Eye View on Empirical Applications
chunks.tex_text:
Throughout this course, we apply ML methods to two problems:

0.48
1. Market Timing

• Forecast the aggregate stock market return over time
• A time-series problem: when is the market expected to do well or poorly?
• Use macroeconomic and valuation predictors to time the market
• Lectures 2, 3, 4, 7, 9

0.48
2. Credit Risk

• Predict loan/mortgage defaults
• Classification problem
• Handle class imbalance
• Build credit scorecards and implement credit assessment and monitoring tools
• Lectures 5, 6, 8

Why these two?

• Cover regression and classification
• Represent major industry applications
• Rich datasets available

chunks.section: A Bird's Eye View on Empirical Applications
chunks.tex_text:
The setup:

• Target: next month's aggregate market excess return, $r_{m,t+1}$
• Predictors: macroeconomic and valuation variables known at time $t$
• A time-series forecasting problem

Typical predictors (15–20 from the literature):

• Valuation ratios: dividend-price, earnings-price, book-to-market
• Interest rates: T-bill rate, term spread, default spread
• Other: stock variance, inflation, net equity expansion

Key questions we will address:

• Can we beat the historical mean out-of-sample?
• Is there genuine economic value in timing the market?
• Do ML methods help, or do they just overfit?

chunks.section: A Bird's Eye View on Empirical Applications
chunks.tex_text:
A return forecast is only useful if it improves decisions.

Typical workflow:

• Forecast next month's market return $\widehat{r}_{m,t+1}$
• Translate the forecast into an equity allocation (e.g., shift between stocks and bonds)
• Rebalance as new information arrives
• Measure performance against a buy-and-hold benchmark

Performance metrics:

• Out-of-sample $R^2$ vs. the historical mean (often tiny — even $\sim$ 0.5% per month is economically meaningful)
• Sharpe ratio of the timing strategy
• Certainty-equivalent / utility gains for a mean-variance investor
• Turnover and transaction costs

Spoiler: Generating genuine out-of-sample economic value (net of transaction costs) is more difficult than you would think.

chunks.section: A Bird's Eye View on Empirical Applications
chunks.tex_text:
The setup:

• Data: LendingClub peer-to-peer loans
• Features: Borrower characteristics, loan terms
• Target: Default (yes/no) — a classification problem

Types of features:

• Borrower: Income, employment length, home ownership
• Credit history: FICO score, delinquencies, credit utilization
• Loan: Amount, interest rate, purpose, term

Key challenges:

• Class imbalance: Defaults are rare ( $\sim$ 10–20%)
• Probability calibration: Need accurate default probabilities
• Interpretability: Regulators require explanations
• Fairness: Cannot discriminate on protected characteristics

chunks.section: A Bird's Eye View on Empirical Applications
chunks.tex_text:
Credit predictions feed into business decisions:

Use cases:

• Approve/reject: Set threshold on predicted default probability
• Pricing: Charge higher interest to riskier borrowers
• Portfolio management: Allocate capital across risk buckets
• Regulatory capital: Calculate required reserves

Performance metrics:

• AUC-ROC (ranking quality)
• Precision/Recall at decision threshold
• Expected loss vs. actual loss
• Calibration: Do 10% predicted defaults actually default 10%?

Reference: Khandani, Kim & Lo (2010) show ML methods significantly outperform traditional credit scorecards.

• ML focuses on prediction; we learn $\widehat{f}$ from data
• Bias-variance tradeoff governs model selection
• Time-series CV is essential for financial applications
• Beware of look-ahead bias, survivorship bias, and data snooping
• Python + scikit-learn provide a consistent ML workflow
• We will apply methods to market timing and credit risk

ML offers powerful tools, but finance demands extra caution. A disciplined and rigorous approach to validation is essential.

Readings for Today's Lecture:

• James et al. (2023), An Introduction to Statistical Learning, Chapters 2 and 5
• Harvey, Liu & Zhu (2016), “ and the Cross-Section of Expected Returns,” Review of Financial Studies
• Gu, Kelly & Xiu (2020), “Empirical Asset Pricing via Machine Learning,” Review of Financial Studies (Introduction and Section 2)
• Chordia, Goyal & Saretto (2020), “Anomalies and False Rejections,” Review of Financial Studies

Week 2 topics:

• OLS limitations in high dimensions
• Ridge regression (L2 penalty)
• Lasso (L1 penalty, sparsity)
• Elastic Net (combining L1 and L2)
• Application: Market Timing and Factor Investing

Last week we established the framework:
\[ Y = f(X) + \epsilon, \qquad \E[\epsilon] = 0 \]

Key insights from Lecture 1:

• We estimate $\widehat{f}$ from training data
• Prediction error = Bias $^2$ + Variance + Irreducible error
• Model complexity creates a trade-off
• Model must be evaluated out-of-sample

This week: What happens when we have many predictors?

Typical setting:

• Many candidate predictors (firm characteristics, macro variables)
• Limited time series (decades, not centuries)
• Low signal-to-noise ratio in returns (noisy returns, weak correlations)

Examples:

• Market timing: 15–20 macro predictors, 50–100 years monthly data
• Factor investing: 100+ firm characteristics, 30–60 years of data

How do we estimate reliably when the number of (possibly weak) predictors $p$ is large relative to sample size $n$ ?

Linear regression model:
\[ y_i = x_i'\beta + \epsilon_i, \qquad i = 1, \ldots, n \]

Notation:

• $y$ : $n \times 1$ vector of outcomes (e.g., returns)
• $X$ : $n \times p$ matrix of predictors (e.g., characteristics, macro variables)
• $\beta$ : $p \times 1$ vector of coefficients (unknown)
• $\epsilon$ : $n \times 1$ vector of errors

In matrix form:
\[ y = X\beta + \epsilon \]

Objective: Minimize sum of squared residuals (RSS)
\[ \widehat{\beta}^{OLS} = \argmin_\beta \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - x_i'\beta)^2 = \argmin_\beta \|y - X\beta\|^2 \]

Closed-form solution:
\[ \widehat{\beta}^{OLS} = (X'X)^{-1}X'y \]

Requires $X'X$ to be invertible (full rank).

Under standard assumptions ( $\E[\epsilon|X] = 0$ , constant variance of $\epsilon$ ):

1. Unbiased:
\[ \E[\widehat{\beta}^{OLS}] = \beta \]

2. Variance:
\[ \Var(\widehat{\beta}^{OLS}) = \sigma^2 (X'X)^{-1} \]

3. Gauss-Markov: Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE)

OLS is optimal when its assumptions hold and $p$ is small relative to $n$ .

chunks.subsection: When OLS Fails — Too Many Predictors
chunks.tex_text:
The problem: More predictors than observations

Example:

• 100 firm characteristics (predictors), 60 months of data (observations)
• OLS cannot find a unique solution

Why? With $p>n$ , the model can fit the data in countless ways.

• Mathematically: $(X'X)$ becomes non-invertible when $p > n$
• Intuitively: Too many “knobs to turn,” not enough data to pin them down

Even when $p < n$ : Correlated predictors cause instability

• Small changes in data $\Rightarrow$ large swings in $\widehat{\beta}$
• Estimates are unreliable

Result: High variance, poor out-of-sample performance

Each orange point: $\widehat{\beta}^{OLS}$ from a different sample.

With many predictors, estimates are scattered around the true ${\color{ThemeBlue}\beta^{true}}$

In-sample: Adding predictors always improves fit

• $R^2$ increases (or stays the same)
• RSS decreases (or stays the same)
• Can achieve a perfect fit if $p = n$

Out-of-sample: Performance often decreases

• Model fits noise, not signal
• Predictions on new data are poor
• May be worse than simple benchmarks

Always evaluate predictive performance out-of-sample.

Key insight: Accept some bias to reduce variance

Recall from Lecture 1:
\[ \MSE(\widehat{\beta}) = \Bias(\widehat{\beta})^2 + \Var(\widehat{\beta}) \]

OLS:

• Zero bias (unbiased)
• High variance when $p$ is large

Regularized methods:

• Shrink coefficients toward zero
• Introduces bias, but substantially reduces variance
• Net effect: Lower total MSE

Intuition: By being “skeptical” of extreme coefficient values, we avoid fitting noise in the training data.

Problem with OLS: Coefficients can be arbitrarily large

Solution: Penalize large coefficients

• Add a “cost” for coefficient magnitude
• Model must balance fit vs. coefficient size
• Large coefficients only if strongly supported by data

Intuition:

• Without penalty: “Use any coefficient values you want”
• With penalty: “Large coefficients are expensive”

Penalized least squares:
\[ \widehat{\beta}^{Ridge} = \argmin_\beta \left\{ \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - x_i'\beta)^2}_{\text{RSS}} + \underbrace{\lambda \sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2}_{\text{Ridge Penalty}} \right\} \]

The penalty term:

• $\sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2 = \|\beta\|_2^2$ is the squared $L_2$ norm
• Penalizes the sum of squared coefficients
• $\lambda \geq 0$ is a penalty parameter that needs to be tuned

The tuning parameter $\lambda$ controls the strength of regularization:

• $\lambda = 0$ : No penalty $\Rightarrow$ recover OLS

• $\lambda \to \infty$ : Infinite penalty $\Rightarrow$ all $\widehat{\beta}_j \to 0$

• Intermediate $\lambda$ : Balance between fit and complexity

$\lambda$ is a hyperparameter — not estimated from the objective function. We choose it separately (e.g., via cross-validation).

Ridge regression can be written as a constrained optimization:
\[ \min_\beta \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - x_i'\beta)^2 \qquad \text{subject to} \qquad \sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2 \leq t \]

Interpretation:

• Minimize RSS subject to a “budget” on coefficients
• Budget $t$ corresponds to penalty $\lambda$ (one-to-one mapping)
• Smaller $t$ $\Leftrightarrow$ larger $\lambda$ $\Leftrightarrow$ more shrinkage

Geometric view: Coefficients must lie within a sphere of radius $\sqrt{t}$ .

• Points with equal RSS (inner = better fit)
• Constraint region (coefficients must stay inside)
• OLS: $\widehat{\beta}^{OLS}$ best fit, ignoring the constraint
• Ridge: $\widehat{\beta}^{Ridge}$ best fit within the constraint

Ridge regression has an analytical solution:
\[ \widehat{\beta}^{Ridge} = (X'X + \lambda I)^{-1}X'y \]

Compare to OLS: $\widehat{\beta}^{OLS} = (X'X)^{-1}X'y$

Key insight: Adding $\lambda I$ to $X'X$ :

• Ensures the matrix is always invertible (even if $p > n$ )
• “Stabilizes” the estimation
• Larger $\lambda$ $\Rightarrow$ more shrinkage toward zero

Practical advantage: Closed-form solution means fast computation, even with many predictors.

Key properties:

• All coefficients are shrunk toward zero
• No coefficient is set exactly to zero (all predictors remain)
• Correlated predictors: Weight is shared more evenly across them

Intuition: Ridge is “skeptical” of large coefficients — requires strong evidence in the data to maintain them.

Financial interpretation:

• Large coefficient = strong belief in predictability
• Shrinkage = disciplined skepticism
• Appropriate given concerns about data mining in finance

Bias: Ridge introduces bias

• $\E[\widehat{\beta}^{Ridge}] \neq \beta$ (biased estimator)
• Coefficients are systematically shrunk toward zero

Variance: Ridge reduces variance

• $\Var(\widehat{\beta}^{Ridge}) < \Var(\widehat{\beta}^{OLS})$
• Shrinkage stabilizes estimates across different samples

MSE tradeoff:

• Small $\lambda$ : Low bias, high variance (close to OLS)
• Large $\lambda$ : High bias, low variance (close to zero)
• Optimal $\lambda$ : Minimizes total MSE
Optimal $\lambda^*$ balances bias and variance to minimize total MSE.

Problem: Ridge penalty depends on coefficient magnitude
\[ \text{Penalty} = \lambda \sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2 \]

If predictors are on different scales (e.g., dollars vs. millions):

• Coefficients $\beta_j$ have different magnitudes
• Penalty affects them unequally

Solution: Standardize each feature before estimation
\[ \tilde{x}_{ij} = \frac{x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j}{s_j} \]

• Each feature has mean zero, standard deviation one
• Coefficients become comparable
• In sklearn: StandardScaler() handles this automatically

Ridge regression:

• Shrinks all coefficients toward zero
• But: Never sets coefficients exactly to zero
• All $p$ predictors remain in the model

When is this a problem?

• Many irrelevant predictors (want to exclude them)
• Desire for interpretability (which variables matter?)
• Sparse models may generalize better out-of-sample

Want: A method that performs variable selection automatically.

Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (Tibshirani, 1996):
\[ \widehat{\beta}^{Lasso} = \argmin_\beta \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - x_i'\beta)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j| \right\} \]

Key difference from Ridge:

• Ridge: $L_2$ penalty $\sum_j \beta_j^2$ (squared coefficients)
• Lasso: $L_1$ penalty $\sum_j |\beta_j|$ (absolute values)

Crucial property: Lasso produces sparse solutions — some $\widehat{\beta}_j = 0$ exactly.

chunks.subsection: The Key Difference: Ridge vs. Lasso Penalties
chunks.tex_text:
p 0.45
Ridge Regression (L2) & Lasso Regression (L1)

(r) 1-1 (l) 2-2
Penalizes squares: $\beta^2$ & Penalizes absolute values: $|\beta|$

&

Shrinks coefficients proportionally & Pushes coefficients toward zero

Never becomes exactly zero & Can become exactly zero

Visualizing the Penalty Effect:

The Analogy:

• is like a rubber band - hard to pull all the way to zero
• has a spring-loaded latch at zero - weak coefficients snap to zero

chunks.subsection: How Lasso Works: The “Soft Thresholding” Rule
chunks.tex_text:
A Simple Decision Rule:

Lasso asks each variable: “Is your signal strong enough to survive the penalty?”

For each coefficient:

• Start with the regular model coefficient ( $\widehat{\beta}_{OLS}$ )
• Subtract the Lasso penalty ( $\lambda$ )
• Apply the decision rule:

• If result is positive $\rightarrow$ keep it
• If result is negative $\rightarrow$ set to zero

Example: (with penalty $\lambda = 0.5$ )

& Step 1: & Step 2: & Step 3:

& $\widehat{\beta}_{OLS}$ & Subtract $\lambda$ & Final Decision

(r) 1-1 (lr) 2-2 (lr) 3-3 (l) 4-4
Variable A & 1.2 & 1.2 - 0.5 = 0.7 & Kept (0.7)

Variable B & 0.4 & 0.4 - 0.5 = -0.1 & Set to ZERO

Variable C & -0.8 & |-0.8| - 0.5 = 0.3 & Kept (-0.3)

Ridge: Proportional shrinkage (all coefficients reduced).

Lasso: Soft thresholding (small coefficients set to zero).

General case: No analytical solution for Lasso

Why?

• The $L_1$ penalty $|\beta_j|$ is not differentiable at zero
• Cannot set derivative to zero and solve

Solution: Iterative optimization algorithms

• Coordinate descent: Update one $\beta_j$ at a time
• Very efficient implementations exist
• sklearn uses fast coordinate descent

Practical implementation: Slightly slower than Ridge, but still fast.

Advantages:

• Automatic variable selection (sparse solutions)
• Interpretability: Identifies “important” predictors
• Can handle $p > n$ (selects at most $n$ variables)

Limitations:

• With correlated predictors: Selects one arbitrarily
• Can be unstable: Different samples $\Rightarrow$ different selections
• At most $\min(n, p)$ non-zero coefficients

Next: what to do when correlation matters but we still want to do variable selection.

Ridge:

• [ $+$ ] Handles correlated predictors well
• [ $+$ ] Stable estimates
• [ $-$ ] No variable selection (keeps all predictors)

Lasso:

• [ $+$ ] Automatic variable selection
• [ $+$ ] Sparse, interpretable models
• [ $-$ ] Unstable with correlated predictors

Elastic Net: Combine both penalties to get the best of both worlds.

Zou & Hastie (2005):
\[ \widehat{\beta}^{EN} = \argmin_\beta \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - x_i'\beta)^2 + \lambda \left[ \alpha \sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j| + (1-\alpha) \sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2 \right] \right\} \]

Two hyperparameters:

• $\lambda \geq 0$ : Overall penalty strength
• $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ : Mixing parameter

• $\alpha = 1$ : Pure Lasso
• $\alpha = 0$ : Pure Ridge
• $\alpha \in (0,1)$ : Combination

Inherits advantages of both methods:

From Lasso ( $L_1$ component):

• Sparse solutions (variable selection)
• Can set coefficients exactly to zero

From Ridge ( $L_2$ component):

• Grouping effect: Correlated predictors selected together
• More stable than pure Lasso
• Can select more than $n$ variables

In practice: Often the best choice for high-dimensional problems.

Method & Penalty & Sparse? & Closed-form? & Correlation

OLS & None & No & Yes & Poor

Ridge & $L_2$ & No & Yes & Good

Lasso & $L_1$ & Yes & No & Poor

Elastic Net & $L_1 + L_2$ & Yes & No & Good

Guidance:

• Few predictors, low correlation $\Rightarrow$ OLS may suffice
• Many correlated predictors $\Rightarrow$ Ridge or Elastic Net
• Want variable selection $\Rightarrow$ Lasso or Elastic Net
• High-dimensional, correlated $\Rightarrow$ Elastic Net

OLS:
\[ \widehat{\beta}^{OLS} = (X'X)^{-1}X'y \]

Ridge:
\[ \widehat{\beta}^{Ridge} = (X'X + \lambda I)^{-1}X'y \]

Lasso:
\[ \widehat{\beta}^{Lasso} = \argmin_\beta \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - x_i'\beta)^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j| \right\} \]

Elastic Net:
\[ \widehat{\beta}^{EN} = \argmin_\beta \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - x_i'\beta)^2 + \lambda \left[ \alpha \sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j| + (1-\alpha) \sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2 \right] \right\} \]

Remember: Ridge, Lasso, Elastic Net all depend on $\lambda$ (and $\alpha$ ).

How do we choose these hyperparameters?

Cannot use training sample:

• Training error always decreases with less regularization
• Would select $\lambda = 0$ (back to OLS)
• Overfits to training data

Need: A proxy for the out-of-sample performance of the model.

K-Fold Cross-Validation

Procedure:

• Split data into K equal parts (“folds”)
• For each fold: train on other K-1 folds, validate the prediction error on this fold
• Average the K prediction errors

Common choice: K = 5 or K = 10

Standard CV: Observations are exchangeable (order does not matter).

Time series reality:

• Observations are ordered in time
• Cannot use future data to predict past

Random splits create look-ahead bias:

• Model trained on 2015–2020 data
• Tested on 2018 data (performance is biased!)

Key principle: Always train on past, test on future.

Training set Test set

Both respect temporal ordering — no look-ahead bias.

• Expanding window: More data as we move forward; assumes stable relationships
• Rolling window: Older data dropped as new data added; adapts to regime changes

Step 1: Define a grid of candidate $\lambda$ values

• Spaced on log scale (factors of 10): $\lambda \in \{0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100\}$

Step 2: For each $\lambda$ , compute time-series CV error

• Use 5-fold time-series CV on training data
• For each fold $k$ : calculate prediction error $\text{MSE}_k(\lambda)$
• Average across folds: $\overline{\text{CV}}(\lambda) = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{k=1}^5 \text{MSE}_k(\lambda)$
• Standard error: $\text{SE}(\lambda) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{5}\sum_{k=1}^5 (\text{MSE}_k(\lambda) - \overline{\text{CV}}(\lambda))^2}$

Step 3: Plot CV error vs. $\lambda$ with error bars

Step 4: Select optimal $\lambda$

• Option A: $\lambda_{\min}$ that minimizes $\overline{\text{CV}}(\lambda)$
• Option B: Largest $\lambda$ where $\overline{\text{CV}}(\lambda) \leq \overline{\text{CV}}(\lambda_{\min}) + \text{SE}(\lambda_{\min})$

chunks.subsection: Understanding the Standard Error in CV
chunks.tex_text:
Why do we need the standard error?

• CV error is computed on 5 different validation folds
• Each fold gives a slightly different error estimate
• The standard error measures variability across folds

Example for Ridge with $\lambda = 10$ :

Fold & MSE

Fold 1 & 0.0042

Fold 2 & 0.0038

Fold 3 & 0.0045

Fold 4 & 0.0041

Fold 5 & 0.0039

Mean & 0.0041

Std. Dev. & 0.00026

Std. Error (SE) & 0.00012

Note: SE = Std. Dev. / $\sqrt{5}$ measures uncertainty in mean CV error
1-SE rule intuition: If $\lambda = 10$ performs within 1 SE of $\lambda = 1$ , the difference could be just noise from CV sampling. Choose the simpler model ( $\lambda = 10$ = more regularization).

Motivation: Protect against overfitting to the validation set

0.48
$\lambda_{\min}$ (minimum CV error):

• [ $+$ ] Best on validation data
• [ $-$ ] May overfit to noise in validation folds
• [ $-$ ] Less regularization
• [ $-$ ] More complex model

0.48
$\lambda_{1SE}$ (1-SE rule):

• [ $+$ ] More conservative
• [ $+$ ] Simpler model (more shrinkage)
• [ $+$ ] Often better on true test data
• [ $-$ ] Slightly worse on validation

• Default: Use $\lambda_{1SE}$ for most applications
• Alternative: Use $\lambda_{\min}$ if you have lots of data and computational budget for extensive CV
• Both: Try both and compare on final test set

Suppose we get these results from 5-fold time-series CV:

$\lambda$ & Mean CV Error & Std Error & Min + 1 SE

0.001 & 0.0052 & 0.00018 & —

0.01 & 0.0048 & 0.00016 & —

0.1 & 0.0044 & 0.00015 & —

1 & 0.0041 & 0.00012 & 0.0041 + 0.00012 = 0.00422

10 & 0.0042 & 0.00013 & —

100 & 0.0046 & 0.00014 & —

• $\lambda_{\min}$ : $\lambda = 1$ has lowest CV error (0.0041)
• 1-SE threshold: 0.0041 + 0.00012 = 0.00422
• $\lambda_{1SE}$ : $\lambda = 10$ has CV error 0.0042 $<$ 0.00422 $\checkmark$
• Higher $\lambda$ (100) exceeds threshold, so choose $\lambda = 10$

Interpretation: $\lambda = 10$ is statistically indistinguishable from $\lambda = 1$ , but simpler (more regularization), so we prefer it.

Central question: Can we predict the stock market?

If YES:

• Time the market (shift allocation between stocks and bonds)
• Significant economic value

If NO:

• Buy and hold is optimal
• Active timing destroys value (transaction costs)

Academic debate: Decades of research, no consensus.

Time-series regression:
\[ r_{m,t+1} = \alpha + x_t'\boldsymbol{\beta} + \epsilon_{t+1} \]
Variables:

• $r_{m,t+1}$ : Market excess return in month $t+1$
• $x_t$ : Vector of predictor variables known at time $t$
• $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ : Coefficients capturing predictive relationships

Goal: Forecast $r_{m,t+1}$ using information available at $t$ .

Classical predictors: $\sim$ 15–20 variables commonly used in the literature

• Valuation ratios: Dividend-price ratio (D/P), Earnings-price ratio (E/P), Book-to-market ratio (B/M)
• Interest rates: Treasury bill rate, Term spread (long minus short rates), Default spread (BAA minus AAA yields)
• Other: Stock variance, Inflation, Net equity expansion

Simplest possible forecast:
\[ \widehat{r}_{t+1} = \bar{r}_t = \frac{1}{t} \sum_{s=1}^{t} r_s \]

Expanding-window average of past returns (Campbell and Thompson 2008).

Economic underpinning:

• No predictability
• Constant expected return
• Ignores all predictor variables

This naive benchmark is surprisingly hard to beat out-of-sample.

Out-of-sample $R^2$ :
\[ R^2_{OS} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{t=t_0}^{T-1}(r_{t+1} - \widehat{r}_{t+1})^2}{\sum_{t=t_0}^{T-1}(r_{t+1} - \bar{r}_{t})^2} \]
Interpretation:

• $R^2_{OS} > 0$ : Model beats the historical mean
• $R^2_{OS} = 0$ : Model equals the historical mean
• $R^2_{OS} < 0$ : Model is worse than the mean

Key difference from in-sample $R^2$ :

• In-sample $R^2 \geq 0$ always
• Out-of-sample $R^2_{OS}$ can be negative!

Comprehensive study of equity premium prediction.

Methodology:

• Test all major predictors from the literature
• Proper out-of-sample evaluation
• Expanding window estimation

Results:

• Most predictors: $R^2_{OS} < 0$
• In-sample significance $\neq$ out-of-sample success
• Historical mean hard to beat

Sobering conclusion: Market timing is very difficult.

Extended sample: Through 2022 (15 more years of data).

Key questions:

• Do original findings hold?
• Have any predictors improved?
• What about new predictors?

Main findings:

• Original conclusions largely confirmed
• Most predictors still fail out-of-sample
• A few show modest improvement
• Still very difficult to beat the mean

chunks.subsection: The Overfitting Problem in Market Timing
chunks.tex_text:
With many predictors:

• OLS finds patterns that fit historical data
• Many of these patterns are spurious (noise)
• Out-of-sample: Spurious patterns don't repeat

Result:

• In-sample $R^2$ can be substantial (5–10%)
• Out-of-sample $R^2_{OS}$ often negative
• Would have been better to predict zero!

Key insight: Regularization provides disciplined skepticism on predictability.

Research Question: Can we predict equity market returns using macroeconomic variables?

• Classic problem in finance: market timing
• Key tension: many predictors vs limited data
• Traditional approach (OLS) typically fails out-of-sample

Compare OLS with regularized methods (Ridge, Lasso, Elastic Net) for predicting monthly excess returns on the S&P 500 using 25 macroeconomic predictors

Key Readings: Goyal, Welch & Zafirov (2024, RFS); Campbell & Thompson (2008, RFS)

Target variable:

• Monthly excess return on S&P 500; Equity Risk Premium (ERP)
• Sample: April 1953 – December 2021 (824 months)

25 Predictors:

• Macroeconomic variables: Debt-to-GDP (dtoy), Inflation (infl), Output gap (ogap), Term spread (tms), Treasury bill rate (tbl), Oil price (wtexas), etc.
• Market variables: Average correlation (avgcor), Book-to-market (b_m), Debt-to-equity (d_e), Dividend-price (d_p), Default spread (dfy), Debt-to-assets (dtoat), Earnings-price (e_p), Net equity issuance (ntis), Stock variance (svar), Skewness (skvw), Tail risk (tail), etc.

Predictive regression: All predictors are known only up to time $t-1$ when forecasting return at $t$

Walk-Forward Predictions: The gold standard for evaluating predictive models in finance.

• Avoids look-ahead bias
• Mimics real-time trading decisions
• Tests true predictive power, not in-sample fit

Empirical Setup:

• Initial training window: 120 months (10 years)
• Move forward: Expand training window, predict next month
• Out-of-sample period: 1967–2023 (672 months)
• Benchmark: Historical mean $\widehat{r}_{t+1} = \frac{1}{t}\sum_{s=1}^t r_s$
• Competing models: Ridge, Lasso, Elastic Net.
• Hyperparameters: Selected via 5-fold time-series cross-validation on training data

Measuring predictive accuracy relative to historical mean:

Model & $R^2_{OS}$ (%)

OLS & $-10.69$

Ridge & $-1.38$

Lasso & $-0.11$

Elastic Net & $-0.17$

Key insight: All models underperform historical mean, but regularization dramatically reduces overfitting (OLS is 100 $\times$ worse than Lasso!)

The chart shows the cumulative Sum of Squared Error (SSE) difference: $\sum_{t=1}^T [(y_t - \widehat{y}_t^{\text{model}})^2 - (y_t - \bar{y}_t)^2]$

• Below zero = model beats historical mean
• Above zero = model worse than historical mean

chunks.subsection: Cumulative SSE Differential: What We Learn
chunks.tex_text:
OLS (blue):

• Starts well in 1970s, then catastrophically diverges
• Post-2008: Accumulates 0.10+ in squared errors vs mean
• Problem: Extreme predictions amplify errors during crises

Ridge (green):

• Stays below zero throughout entire period
• Occasionally beats mean (1980s-90s, 2020s)
• Most stable predictor – never catastrophically wrong

Lasso/Elastic Net (orange/red):

• Track historical mean closely (near zero line)
• Slight improvement in the 2020s
• Conservative: avoid big losses but also miss opportunities

Recessions (gray bars): All models struggle, but Ridge degrades least

• Market returns are extremely difficult to predict
• Even small forecast errors are heavily penalized
• Historical mean is a surprisingly strong benchmark
• This is typical in equity premium prediction literature

What matters:

• Relative performance: Regularized models much closer to zero
• Economic value: Can poor $R^2$ still generate profits?
• Directional accuracy: Getting the sign right matters more than magnitude

Getting the direction right is crucial for market timing:

Model & Sign Acc. (%) & When Up (%) & When Down (%)

Historical Mean & 59.4 & 100.0 & 0.0

OLS & 57.8 & 68.7 & 42.0

Ridge & 59.5 & 73.9 & 38.5

Lasso & 59.2 & 99.8 & 0.0

Elastic Net & 58.8 & 94.3 & 7.0

Observations:

• Ridge has best overall sign accuracy (59.5%)
• Lasso/Elastic Net behaves like the historical mean (always bullish)
• OLS is more balanced but least accurate overall
• Important: 59.5% accuracy can be economically valuable!

Market timing strategy: Allocate between equity and risk-free asset

Mean-variance optimal weight:
\[ \omega_t = \frac{1}{\gamma}\frac{\widehat{r}_{t+1}}{\widehat{\sigma}^2_t} \]
where:

• $\widehat{r}_{t+1}$ = model's predicted excess return
• $\gamma = 5$ = risk aversion coefficient
• $\widehat{\sigma}^2_t$ = realized variance (rolling 60-month window)
• Constraints: $-1 \leq \omega_t \leq 1.5$ (allow 50% leverage, 100% cash)

Portfolio return:
\[ r_{t+1}^{\text{portfolio}} = \omega_t \cdot r_{t+1}^{\text{market}} + (1-\omega_t) \cdot r_f \]

What are we looking at? Histogram of equity weights over 672 months

• OLS and Ridge weights often at constraints ( $\omega = -1$ or $\omega = 1.5$ )
• Lasso weights resembles the historical mean's: strong L1 penalty often zeros out most coefficients $\Rightarrow$ forecast $\approx$ mean
• E-net is between Lasso and Ridge, with weights more dispersed around 0.75

Gross performance metrics:

After 50 bps transaction costs for each rebalancing month:

Takeaway: Ridge achieves best risk-adjusted returns even after costs

Left panel: Gross returns (no transaction costs)
Right panel: Net returns (50 bps costs)

After 50 bps (0.5%) transaction costs per trade:

OLS collapses:

• \$158 $\rightarrow$ \$15
• High turnover (7.8 $\times$ per year) $\Rightarrow$ costs destroy value
• Lesson: Overconfident predictions $\Rightarrow$ excessive trading

Ridge survives:

• \$320 $\rightarrow$ \$49
• Moderate turnover (6.3 $\times$ per year) is still costly, but tolerable
• Still beats buy-and-hold after costs!

Historical Mean/Lasso/Elastic Net:

• Largely unchanged (low turnover $<$ 2 $\times$ per year)
• But still underperform buy-and-hold

chunks.subsection: Key Lessons for ML in Finance
chunks.tex_text:
• Regularization is essential

• OLS catastrophically overfits with many predictors (despite $p<<n$ ).
• Ridge provides the best balance: shrinkage without extreme sparsity

• Statistical vs economic performance diverge

• Negative $R^2_{OS}$ does not mean no economic value
• 59.5% directional accuracy $\Rightarrow$ 11% annualized return

• Transaction costs matter enormously

• High-turnover strategies (OLS) lose appeal after costs
• More stable predictions (Ridge) better preserve value

• Walk-forward testing is non-negotiable

• In-sample $R^2$ would be misleading
• Real-time prediction reveals true model performance

What we learned about deployment:

• Feature engineering: Use lagged predictors to avoid look-ahead bias

• Predict $r_{t+1}$ using $X_t$ (known at time $t$ )
• Easy to get wrong – always validate timing!

• Hyperparameter tuning: Use time-series cross-validation

• Never shuffle time series data
• Refit periodically as markets evolve

• Scaling: Standardize features for regularized methods

• Ridge/Lasso/Elastic Net need consistent scales
• OLS is scale-invariant (no scaling needed)

• Constraints: Impose realistic portfolio constraints

• Leverage limits, no-short-selling, etc.
• Unconstrained weights can be extreme and unrealistic

Market timing: When to invest?

• Predict aggregate market return
• Shift between stocks and bonds

Cross-sectional prediction: Which stocks to pick?

• Predict relative performance across stocks
• Long winners, short losers

Key difference:

• Same methods, different data structure
• Cross-section: Much more data (many stocks)
• Often more successful than market timing

Traditional approach:

• Identify a characteristic (e.g., book-to-market)
• Rank stocks by that characteristic
• Long high-ranked stocks, short low-ranked
• Earn the “factor premium”

Examples:

• Value: High book-to-market outperforms
• Momentum: Past winners continue to win
• Size: Small stocks outperform (historically)
• Profitability: Profitable firms outperform

Question: Which characteristics? How to combine them?

Cross-sectional regression:
\[ r_{i,t+1} = x_{i,t}'\boldsymbol{\beta} + \epsilon_{i,t+1} \]

Interpretation:

• Characteristics $x_{i,t}$ predict returns at $t+1$
• $\beta_j$ : Premium associated with characteristic $j$
• Linear combination of characteristics $\Rightarrow$ expected return

Panel structure: Observations are stock-months.

• Many more observations ( $N \times T$ vs. $T$ )
• More power to detect patterns (number of stocks $N$ can be large)

Challenge:

• Many candidate characteristics ( $p$ large)
• Characteristics are correlated
• Which ones really matter?

Harvey, Liu & Zhu (2016):

• 300+ factors published in academic journals
• Most discovered through data mining
• Many are likely spurious

Gu, Kelly & Xiu (2020):

• 900+ firm characteristics
• Comprehensive dataset for ML

Problem:

• Too many predictors for OLS
• Many are correlated (size, value, profitability overlap)
• Need principled way to select/combine

Traditional approach:

• Select a small number of characteristics
• Justify selection with theory or prior evidence
• Problem: Which ones? Selection is arbitrary.

Kozak, Nagel & Santosh (KNS) approach:

• Use all $100+$ characteristics
• Apply Ridge regression to handle high dimensionality
• Let shrinkage determine relative importance

Key insight: Ridge shrinkage is equivalent to imposing a prior belief that most characteristics have small effects.

Procedure:

• Estimate $\widehat{\beta}$ using data up to month $t$

• For each stock $i$ , compute predicted return:
\[ \widehat{r}_{i,t+1} = x_{i,t}'\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \]

• Rank stocks by $\widehat{r}_{i,t+1}$

• Form portfolio:

• Long: Top decile (highest predicted returns)
• Short: Bottom decile (lowest predicted returns)

• Observe realized portfolio return in month $t+1$

• Roll forward, repeat

Re-estimate periodically (monthly or quarterly).

Track cumulative returns.

Kozak, Nagel & Santosh (2020) findings:

• Ridge substantially outperforms OLS out-of-sample

• Optimal $\lambda$ implies significant shrinkage

• Data supports skepticism about many characteristics

• Long-short portfolios achieve Sharpe ratios $> 1$

• Results robust across different sample periods

• Better performance than selecting ad-hoc subsets

Key message: Regularization is not just a technical fix — it embodies economically sensible skepticism.

• Do not search for the “true” sparse model

• Financial markets are complex; many factors matter
• Embrace high-dimensional methods rather than fight dimensionality

• Consider Ridge as baseline regularization method

• Especially when predictors are correlated (common in finance)
• More robust than Lasso or OLS
• Can always add L $_1$ penalty if you need interpretability

• Always validate out-of-sample

• In-sample fit is misleading with many predictors
• Use proper time-series validation (walk-forward, cross-validation)
• Test economic value, not just statistical fit

1. Transaction costs

• ML strategies can have high turnover
• Trading costs erode returns
• May need to constrain turnover

2. Capacity constraints

• ML strategies tend to work best in small/mid caps
• Large trades move prices (market impact)
• May not scale to institutional size (liquidity matters)

3. Data quality

• Point-in-time data essential (avoid look-ahead)
• Survivorship bias: Include delisted firms
• Data errors can drive spurious results

• Market timing is very difficult with linear models — even regularization often helps only modestly

• Monthly return volatility $\approx 4$ – $5\%$
• Predictable component $\approx 0.5\%$ (if any)
• Signal buried in noise
• The relationship between ERP and the state of the economy might be non-linear

• Cross-sectional prediction benefits more clearly from shrinkage

• Shrinkage = Skepticism about predictability
• Economically sensible assumption given market timing evidence

• Always evaluate the economic benefit of your model above and beyond statistical accuracy

Readings for Today's Lecture:

• Goyal, Welch, & Zafirov (2024). “A comprehensive 2022 look at the empirical performance of equity premium prediction." The Review of Financial Studies.[Recomm.]
• Kozak, Nagel & Santosh (2020), “Shrinking the Cross-Section,” Journal of Financial Economics[Supp.]

Topics for the next lecture:

• Tree-based methods and ensemble learning

• Random Forests: Bagging + feature sampling
• Gradient Boosting: XGBoost, LightGBM
• Non-linear relationships and interactions

• Key advantage: Automatically capture non-linearities and interactions that linear models miss.

• Can trees resurrect market timing and time-series predictability?

Lecture 2: Given that $f(X) = X^\top\boldsymbol{\beta}$ , how do we estimate $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ well?

• OLS: Unbiased but high variance in high dimensions (overfitting)

• Ridge: Shrinks coefficients toward zero (bias for variance)

• Lasso: Shrinks + selects variables (sparse solutions)

• Elastic Net: Combines L1 and L2 penalties

All methods assume the true relationship is linear in the set of features

This Lecture: What if $f(X) \neq X^\top\boldsymbol{\beta}$ ? How do we let the data reveal the functional form?

Limitations of linear models:

• Linearity: Effect of $X_j$ on $Y$ is constant across all values of $X_j$

• Additivity: Effect of $X_j$ does not depend on values of other predictors

• Global model: Same coefficients everywhere in feature space

We can add polynomials ( $X_j^2$ ) and interactions ( $X_j\cdot X_i$ ) manually, but:

• Which ones? How many?
• Model dimension explodes with many predictors

Goyal-Welch setting: Predict excess market return using macro predictors

• Non-linear effects:

• Dividend yield may predict differently at extremes
• Valuation ratios: effect may saturate

• Interactions matter:

• D/P predicts better when term spread is high?
• Inflation interacts with real rates

• Regime dependence:

• Predictability varies: recessions vs. expansions
• Different models for different market states

Can we let the data discover which predictors matter where?

Core idea: Partition the feature space into regions, fit simple models within each region

0.45
Linear Model:
\[ \widehat{f}(x) = \widehat{\beta}_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \widehat{\beta}_j x_j \]

• Global: same $\beta$ everywhere
• Smooth predictions
• Extrapolates beyond data

0.45
Tree Model:
\[ \widehat{f}(x) = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \widehat{c}_m \cdot \mathbf{1}(x \in R_m) \]

• Local: different prediction by averaging returns in a given region $R_m$
• Piecewise constant
• Cannot extrapolate

Important Trade-off: Flexibility vs. smoothness

• Internal nodes: Binary split on one variable
• Terminal nodes (leaves): Predict constant $\widehat{c}_m = \overline{y}_{R_m}$ (mean of training $y$ in region)

A regression tree partitions feature space into $M$ disjoint regions $R_1, \ldots, R_M$

Prediction function:
\[ \widehat{f}(x) = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \widehat{c}_m \cdot \mathbf{1}(x \in R_m) \]

Optimal constant within each region:
\[ \widehat{c}_m = \frac{1}{n_m} \sum_{x_i \in R_m} y_i = \overline{y}_{R_m} \]

(minimizes squared error within region)

Given the partition $\{R_m\}$ , the optimal predictions are just regional averages

Ideal objective: Find partition minimizing total RSS
\[ \min_{\{R_m\}_{m=1}^{M}} \sum_{m=1}^{M} \sum_{x_i \in R_m} (y_i - \widehat{c}_m)^2 \]

Problem: Computationally infeasible to search all possible partitions

Solution: Greedy recursive binary splitting

• Start with all data in one region
• Find the single best split (one variable, one threshold)
• Repeat recursively on each resulting region
• Stop when criterion met (e.g., minimum leaf size).

Pick the best split right now without looking ahead—like always taking the immediate shortcut without checking if a longer route would be faster overall

At each node, find split $(j, s)$ minimizing:
\[ \min_{j, s} \left[ \sum_{x_i \in R_1(j,s)} (y_i - \widehat{c}_1)^2 + \sum_{x_i \in R_2(j,s)} (y_i - \widehat{c}_2)^2 \right] \]

where:
\begin{align*} R_1(j,s) &= \{X \mid X_j \leq s\} \quad \text{(left child)} \\ R_2(j,s) &= \{X \mid X_j > s\} \quad \text{(right child)} \end{align*}

Algorithm:

• For each predictor $j = 1, \ldots, p$ :

• For each unique value $s$ of $X_j$ in the data:
• Compute RSS reduction from split $(j, s)$

• Select $(j^*, s^*)$ with largest RSS reduction

Complexity: $O(np)$ per split (efficient!)

chunks.subsection: Visual Example: Equity Premium with Two Predictors
chunks.tex_text:
Setup: Predict excess return $r_{t+1}$ using D/P (dividend-price) and TMS (term spread)

Question: How should we partition this space to predict returns?

Algorithm searches all variables and thresholds:

• Best split: $\text{D/P} \leq 2.0$

0.52

0.52

$R_1$ : Low D/P

(mostly negative returns)

$R_2$ : High D/P

(mostly positive returns)

Now split $R_1$ (low D/P region):

• Best split: $\text{TMS} \leq 1.5$

0.55

0.6

$R_1$ : Low D/P, Low TMS (predict: $-2\%$ )

$R_2$ : Low D/P, High TMS (predict: $+1\%$ )

$R_3$ : High D/P (predict: $+3\%$ )

Key properties:

• Splits are always perpendicular to axes (one variable at a time)
• Regions are rectangular (in any dimension)
• Prediction is constant within each region

0.5
The fitted tree says:

• High D/P $\rightarrow$ positive returns
• Low D/P alone $\rightarrow$ depends on TMS
• Low D/P + Low TMS $\rightarrow$ negative returns
• Low D/P + High TMS $\rightarrow$ mildly positive

Interaction discovered:

TMS matters only when D/P is low

0.5
Compare to linear model:
\[ \widehat{r}_{t+1} = \widehat{\beta}_0 + \widehat{\beta}_1 \text{D/P} + \widehat{\beta}_2 \text{TMS} \]

• Effects are additive
• Same $\beta_2$ everywhere
• Would need to specify $\text{D/P} \times \text{TMS}$ interaction manually

• Non-linear effects (different predictions at different D/P levels)
• Interactions (TMS effect depends on D/P)

Stopping rules (hyperparameters):

• Minimum samples per leaf (min_samples_leaf)

• Stop if split would create node with fewer than $k$ observations
• Typical: 1–20 for regression

• Maximum depth (max_depth)

• Limit number of sequential splits
• Typical: 3–20

• Minimum impurity decrease (min_impurity_decrease)

• Stop if RSS reduction below threshold

Problem: Early stopping can miss good splits that only become valuable after further splitting

Better approach: Grow large tree, then prune back

Cost-complexity criterion:
\[ C_\alpha(T) = \sum_{m=1}^{|T|} \sum_{x_i \in R_m} (y_i - \widehat{c}_m)^2 + \alpha |T| \]

• $|T|$ = number of terminal nodes (tree complexity)
• $\alpha \geq 0$ = complexity penalty (tuning parameter)

Lasso: & $\RSS + \lambda \sum_j |\beta_j|$

Tree: & $\RSS + \alpha |T|$

Both trade off fit vs. complexity; $\alpha$ selected via cross-validation

0.48
Deep trees:

• Many small regions
• Low bias (can fit complex patterns)
• High variance (sensitive to training data)
• Risk: overfitting

0.48
Shallow trees:

• Few large regions
• High bias (may miss patterns)
• Low variance (stable)
• Risk: underfitting

Key problem: Single trees are notoriously unstable

• Small change in data $\rightarrow$ completely different tree structure
• Different split at root cascades to entire tree

Can we reduce variance while keeping flexibility?

Illustration: Same data, two different root splits

0.48
Sample A

0.48
Sample B

• High variance in predictions

• Predictions are very sensitive to root splits

• This is the cost of flexibility

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.tex_text:
Think about macroeconomic forecasting:

• One agency forecast: 60% accuracy
• Average forecast from 10 independent agencies: 75% accuracy
• Why? Individual forecasting errors tend to cancel out

The Statistical Principle:

Given $B$ independent observations $z_1, \ldots, z_B$ , each with variance $\sigma^2$ :
\begin{align} \text{Var}(\bar{z}) = \text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^B z_b\right) = \frac{\sigma^2}{B}\notag \end{align}

Averaging reduces variance by a factor of $B$ !

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.subsection: The Challenge: We Don't Have Multiple Datasets
chunks.tex_text:
The Problem:

• Ideally, we'd train trees on $B$ different training datasets
• Then average their predictions: $\widehat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^B \widehat{f}^b(x)$
• But we only have one dataset!

The Solution: Bootstrap Sampling

• Take repeated samples with replacement from our training dataset
• Each bootstrap sample has the same size as the original data
• This creates the variation we need for ensemble diversity

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.tex_text:
Bootstrap: Sample $n$ rows (time periods) with replacement from original data

0.55
What happens:

• Some periods appear multiple times
• Some periods are omitted entirely
• All $p$ predictors remain

0.45
Why it helps:

• Each tree sees different data
• Trees make different errors
• Averaging reduces variance

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.tex_text:
Setup: $B$ estimators, each with variance $\sigma^2$ and pairwise correlation $\rho$

Variance of average:
\[ \Var\left(\widehat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x)\right)=\Var\left(\frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} \widehat{f}^b(x)\right) = \rho\sigma^2 + \frac{1-\rho}{B}\sigma^2 \]

As $B \to \infty$ Variance $\to \rho\sigma^2$

Implications:

• First term depends on correlation between trees
• If $\rho = 0$ : variance $\to 0$ (ideal)
• If $\rho = 1$ : no benefit from averaging
• Issue: Bootstrap trees are correlated (same predictors across trees)

The Challenge: How do we ensure our bagged trees are sufficiently different from each other?

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.subsection: Two Sources of Randomness in Random Forests
chunks.tex_text:
Random Forests combine two randomization strategies:

0.52
ThemeBlue 1. Bootstrap rows (Bagging)

• Sample $n$ time periods with replacement
• Some periods repeated, others omitted
• $\approx 37\%$ left out (“out-of-bag”)
• Goal: Create diverse training sets

0.48
AccentOrange 2. Subsample columns (Random Forest)

• At each split, consider only $m < p$ predictors
• Different $m$ predictors each tree
• All rows in node still used
• Purpose: Decorrelate trees

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.tex_text:
Problem: Even with bootstrap sampling, trees are correlated

Available Chosen for split Not available

Result: Lower correlation $\rho$ between trees $\Rightarrow$ lower $\Var\left(\widehat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x)\right)$

Typical: $m \approx \sqrt{p}$

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.tex_text:
Recall: We bootstrap time periods (rows) with replacement

OOB prediction for observation $i$ :
\[ \widehat{y}_i^{\text{OOB}} = \frac{1}{|B_i|} \sum_{b \in B_i} T_b(x_i) \]
where $B_i = \{b : \text{observation } i \text{ was OOB for tree } b\}$ .

Example: Only Tree 2 and 3 see observation $\mathbf{t_3}$ $\Rightarrow$ OOB prediction using Tree 1 (which did not use $\mathbf{t_3}$ ).

OOB error $\approx$ test error, no need for separate validation set

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.subsection: Summary: The Key Innovation of Random Forest
chunks.tex_text:
Problem: Bagged trees are correlated (few strong predictors can dominate the splits)

Solution: Decorrelate the trees (Breiman, 2001)

Random Forest modification:

At each split, consider only a random subset of $m$ predictors

• Before: choose best split among all $p$ predictors
• After: choose best split among random $m < p$ predictors
• Different random subset at each split

Recall: $\Var\left(\widehat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x)\right) = \rho\sigma^2 + \frac{1-\rho}{B}\sigma^2$

• Reducing $\rho$ directly reduces variance
• May slightly increase individual tree variance $\sigma^2$ (weaker splits)
• Net effect: substantial variance reduction

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.tex_text:
0.48
Bagging / Random Forest

• Build deep trees in parallel
• One tree does not depend on another
• Combine by averaging
• Goal: Variance reduction

0.48
Boosting

• Build shallow trees sequentially
• Each tree depends on previous
• Combine by weighted sum
• Goal: Bias reduction

Bagging: “Average many overfitting trees to reduce variance”

Boosting: “Sequentially correct errors of underfitting trees to reduce bias”

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.tex_text:
Intuition: Fit trees to residuals from previous trees

• Fit a simple tree $\widehat{f}_1(x)$ to data
• Compute residuals: $r_i^{(1)} = y_i - \widehat{f}_1(x_i)$
• Fit next tree $\widehat{f}_2(x)$ to residuals $r^{(1)}$
• Update: $\widehat{f}(x) = \widehat{f}_1(x) + \widehat{f}_2(x)$
• Repeat: fit trees to remaining residuals

Final prediction:
\[ \widehat{f}(x) = \sum_{b=1}^{B} \widehat{f}_b(x) \]

Each tree “boosts” the model by correcting mistakes of all previous trees

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.tex_text:
View as gradient descent in function space (Friedman, 2001)

Objective: Minimize loss $L(y, f(x))$ , e.g., squared error
\[ \min_f \sum_{i=1}^{n} L(y_i, f(x_i)) = \min_f \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - f(x_i))^2 \]

For squared error, the negative gradient is just the residual: $r_i = y_i - f(x_i)$

Concrete example: Each tree tries to predict the previous residual

Iter & Tree target & Tree prediction & Cumulative $\widehat{f}(x_i)$ & New residual

0 & — & — & 2% (mean) & $5\% - 2\% = 3\%$

1 & 3% & $h_1(x_i) = 1.5\%$ & $2\% + 1.5\% = 3.5\%$ & $5\% - 3.5\% = 1.5\%$

2 & 1.5% & $h_2(x_i) = 0.7\%$ & $3.5\% + 0.7\% = 4.2\%$ & $5\% - 4.2\% = 0.8\%$

3 & 0.8% & $h_3(x_i) = 0.4\%$ & $4.2\% + 0.4\% = 4.6\%$ & $5\% - 4.6\% = 0.4\%$

Each tree corrects remaining errors $\rightarrow$ residuals shrink toward zero

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.tex_text:
algorithm[H]
Gradient Boosting for Regression
algorithmic[1]
Initialize $\widehat{f}^{(0)}(x) = \bar{y}$ (mean of training targets)
$b = 1$ to $B$
Compute residuals: $r_i = y_i - \widehat{f}^{(b-1)}(x_i)$ for all $i$
Fit a shallow tree $h_b(x)$ to residuals $\{(x_i, r_i)\}_{i=1}^n$
Update: $\widehat{f}^{(b)}(x) = \widehat{f}^{(b-1)}(x) + \nu \cdot h_b(x)$
Output: $\widehat{f}(x) = \widehat{f}^{(B)}(x) = \bar{y} + \nu \sum_{b=1}^{B} h_b(x)$
algorithmic
algorithm

Key hyperparameters:

• $B$ : number of trees (iterations)
• $\nu \in (0,1]$ : learning rate (shrinkage) — typically 0.01–0.1
• Tree depth: typically 3–8 (shallow!)

Small steps prevent overshooting—like turning down the volume on each correction so we do not over-correct and start fitting noise

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.tex_text:
Recall bias-variance:

• Random Forest: deep trees (low bias) + averaging (reduces variance)
• Boosting: shallow trees (high bias) + sequential correction (reduces bias)

Shallow trees (“stumps” to depth 4–6):

• High bias individually
• But sequential fitting systematically reduces bias
• Low variance $\rightarrow$ stable at each step

Tree depth controls interaction order:

• Depth 1 (stump): only main effects

• Example: “If D/P $> 2$ , predict $+2\%$ ” — no interactions

• Depth 2: pairwise interactions

• Example: “If D/P $> 2$ and TMS $> 1$ , predict $+4\%$ ”

• Depth $d$ : up to $d$ -way interactions

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.tex_text:
Key difference from Random Forest:

• RF: more trees $\rightarrow$ always helps (or neutral)
• Boosting: more trees $\rightarrow$ can eventually overfit

Why? Each boosting tree is designed to fix remaining errors—eventually it starts fitting noise rather than signal

Solution: Monitor validation error, stop when it increases

chunks.section: Ensemble Learning: Bagging, Random Forests, and Boosting
chunks.subsection: RF vs. Gradient Boosting: When to Use Which?
chunks.tex_text:
& Random Forest & Gradient Boosting

Tree construction & Parallel (independent) & Sequential (dependent)

Individual trees & Deep (overfit) & Shallow (underfit)

Ensemble goal & Variance reduction & Bias reduction

Overfitting risk & Low & Higher (use early stopping)

Tuning difficulty & Easier & Harder

Practical guidance:

Limited tuning time: & Start with RF (robust to default parameters)

Maximizing performance: & Try Boosted trees with careful tuning

Very noisy data: & RF (harder to overfit)

Clear signal, many features: & Gradient boosting often wins

Hint: Try both, compare out-of-sample performance

chunks.subsection: Recap: What We Learned in Lecture 2
chunks.tex_text:
Market timing with linear methods:

• OLS overfits badly with many predictors (negative $R^2_{OS}$ )
• Ridge provided best statistical accuracy ( $R^2_{OS}$ closest to zero)
• Lasso/Elastic Net behaved like historical mean (always bullish)
• Economic performance $\neq$ statistical accuracy

Can tree-based methods capture non-linearities and interactions that linear models miss?

Motivation: Maybe D/P predicts differently at extremes, or the effect of TMS depends on inflation regime

Potential advantages over linear models:

• Automatic non-linearity: No need to specify polynomial terms

• Automatic interactions: Discovers D/P $\times$ TMS effects without manual coding

• No scaling required: Trees are scale-invariant

Potential disadvantages:

• Higher variance (especially single trees)
• Hard to extrapolate beyond training data
• More hyperparameters to tune
• Not particularly effective with noisy data

Empirical question: Do these advantages outweigh disadvantages for market timing?

Linear methods (from Lecture 2):

• OLS, Ridge, Lasso, Elastic Net

Tree-based methods (new):

• Decision Tree: Single pruned tree
• Random Forest: Bagging + feature subsampling
• Gradient Boosting: Sequential residual fitting

Common setup:

• Walk-forward prediction (expanding window)
• Initial training: 120 months (10 years)
• Hyperparameters: Time-series cross-validation

Data: GWZ (2024) — 25 predictors, monthly excess returns 1953–2021

Key hyperparameters for regularization:

Parameter & Effect & Grid

max_depth & Limits tree depth & \ 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\

min_samples_leaf & Min. observations per leaf & \ 5, 10, 20\

Why constrain depth?

• The deeper the tree, the more the model overfits
• Shallow trees = simpler, more stable rules
• Market timing: probably only need 2–3 key fundamental interactions

Selection: Time-series cross-validation (no shuffling!)

Key hyperparameters:

Parameter & Effect & Grid

n_estimators & Number of trees & 100 (fixed)

max_depth & Individual tree depth & \ 3, 5, 7, None\

max_features & Features per split & \ $\sqrt{p}$ , 0.33, 0.5\

min_samples_leaf & Min. observations per leaf & \ 5, 10, 20\

Key insight: More trees never hurts (just slower)

Focus tuning on:

• max_features: controls tree correlation (lower $\to$ more diverse)
• max_depth: controls individual tree complexity (deeper tree $\to$ overfitting)

Key hyperparameters:

Parameter & Effect & Grid

n_estimators & Number of boosting rounds & \ 50, 100, 200\

max_depth & Tree depth (interaction order) & \ 2, 3, 4\

learning_rate & Shrinkage factor $\nu$ & \ 0.01, 0.05, 0.1\

Critical trade-off: learning_rate $\times$ n_estimators

• Small $\nu$ + many trees = careful, gradual learning
• Large $\nu$ + few trees = aggressive, faster learning
• Small $\nu$ generally better but slower

Depth for GBM: Keep shallow individual trees (2–4) since boosting reduces bias

Recall: $R^2_{OS} = 1 - \frac{\sum_t(r_t - \widehat{r}_t)^2}{\sum_t(r_t - \bar{r}_t)^2}$

Negative $R^2_{OS}$ means worse than predicting the historical mean.

Model & $R^2_{OS}$ (%) & Assessment

OLS & $-13.85$ & Severe overfitting

Ridge & $-0.50$ & Slight underperformance

Lasso & $+0.10$ & $\approx$ Historical mean

Elastic Net & $+0.14$ & $\approx$ Historical mean

Decision Tree & $-10.68$ & Severe overfitting

Random Forest & $\mathbf{+0.15}$ & Best

Gradient Boosting & $-0.88$ & Moderate overfitting

Surprise: Random Forest achieves the only positive $R^2_{OS}$ among complex models!

Key observations:

• Single trees overfit dramatically ( $R^2_{OS} = -10.7\%$ )

• Worse than OLS in relative terms
• Flexibility without ensemble averaging $\to$ noise fitting

• Random Forest achieves best statistical accuracy

• Only non-linear model with positive $R^2_{OS}$ (+0.15%)
• Averaging + decorrelation effectively controls variance

• Gradient Boosting disappoints ( $R^2_{OS} = -0.88\%$ )

• Sequential bias reduction less useful when signal is weak
• Easier to overfit than Random Forest

• Lasso and E-Net behave like historical mean

• Shrink most coefficients to zero $\to$ predict near-constant

The chart shows the cumulative Sum of Squared Error (SSE) difference: $\sum_{t=1}^T [(y_t - \widehat{y}_t^{\text{model}})^2 - (y_t - \bar{y}_t)^2]$

• Below zero = model beats historical mean
• Above zero = model worse than historical mean

chunks.subsection: Cumulative SSE Differential: What We Learn
chunks.tex_text:
Key patterns in the figure:

• OLS & Decision Tree: Steadily accumulate errors, especially during crises
• Ridge & Random Forest: Fluctuate but consistently below zero
• Lasso/Elastic Net/GBM: Flat near zero (predictions $\approx$ historical mean)

Timing insight: Large jumps occur during volatile periods (gray bands = recessions)

• 2008–2009 crisis: OLS predictions particularly bad

chunks.subsection: Sign Accuracy: Getting the Direction Right
chunks.tex_text:
Sign accuracy: % of months where predicted sign = actual sign

Key insight: Lasso achieves highest sign accuracy but is always bullish—same strategy as historical mean.

chunks.subsection: Why Trees Have More Balanced Predictions
chunks.tex_text:
Sparse linear models (Lasso/Elastic Net):

• Shrink most/all coefficients toward zero
• Predictions collapse to near-constant (historical mean)
• “When Down” accuracy $\approx 0$ –7% (almost never predict negative)

Tree models:

• “When Down” accuracy 13–31% (do predict some negative months)

OLS/Ridge (unconstrained):

• Most balanced: “When Down” accuracy 36–43%
• But also most volatile predictions $\to$ more trading

More balanced predictions $\to$ potentially catch downturns

But also $\to$ higher turnover and more false alarms

Market timing strategy: Same as Lecture 2

Mean-variance optimal weight:
\[ \omega_t = \frac{1}{\gamma}\frac{\widehat{r}_{t+1}}{\widehat{\sigma}^2_t} \]

• $\gamma = 5$ (risk aversion)
• $\widehat{\sigma}^2_t$ = rolling 60-month variance
• Constraints: $-1 \leq \omega_t \leq 1.5$

Portfolio return:
\[ r_{t+1}^{\text{portfolio}} = \omega_t \cdot r_{t+1}^{\text{market}} + (1-\omega_t) \cdot r_f \]

Transaction costs: 50 bps per unit weight change

Key finding: Ridge dominates economically despite worse $R^2_{OS}$ than RF!

Puzzle: How can Ridge have negative $R^2_{OS}$ but best returns?

Turnover comparison:

Observations:

• Tree methods have 3–4 $\times$ annual turnover
• Ridge has highest turnover but still best net Sharpe!
• GBM hurt most by costs (Sharpe drops from 0.36 to 0.22)

Left panel: Gross returns (no transaction costs)
Right panel: Net returns (50 bps costs)

chunks.subsection: Cumulative Returns: What the Figure Shows
chunks.tex_text:
Gross returns (left panel):

• Ridge: Dominates dramatically, reaches \$400+ by 2021
• OLS: Second best gross ( $\sim$ \$290), but very volatile path
• Random Forest: Solid growth ( $\sim$ \$32), much smoother than OLS
• All others: Cluster near bottom (\$5–\$20)

Net returns after 50 bps costs (right panel):

• Ridge: Still best ( $\sim$ \$85), but gap narrows substantially
• Buy & Hold: Catches up to $\sim$ \$40 (no trading costs)
• OLS: Falls to $\sim$ \$40 (high turnover penalized)
• Random Forest: $\sim$ \$10, penalized by 3.85 $\times$ turnover

Ridge's economic dominance persists after costs, but the margin shrinks.

Transaction costs matter more for tree methods than linear methods.

Puzzle: Random Forest has best $R^2_{OS}$ (+0.15%), but Ridge has best returns. Why?

Resolution: $R^2_{OS}$ measures average squared error, but economic value depends on:

• Magnitude of correct predictions

• Ridge makes bigger bets when confident
• RF predictions are more muted (closer to zero)

• Timing of errors

• Being wrong in calm markets costs less than in volatile markets
• Ridge may time the big moves better

• Portfolio construction

• Mean-variance weights amplify prediction differences
• Small differences in predictions $\to$ big differences in positions

Statistical accuracy $\neq$ Economic value. Always evaluate both!
